title: Individual Differences in Human-Robot Interaction: Who Adapts to Changing Automation, and How?
target_journal: Undecided (Organization Science, ASQ, AMJ, or HRI venue)
data_source: JointBot: 26.7M second-by-second HRI phase records, 79 named operators, 16 months (Nov 2017-Feb 2019), shared with Developmental Uncertainty paper

events.version_name: Scoping: Individual Variation in Automation Adaptation

categories.framing: Three candidate framings: (A) Human Capital Formation Under Automation, (B) Individual Adaptability to Technological Change, (C) Cohort Effects in Technology Adoption
categories.mechanism: Undecided — entry timing and individual characteristics predict divergent behavioral adaptation to maturing automation
categories.evidence: 26.7M HRI records, 79 operators, 51 with ≥10 hours. Skill-building time ranges 5.8%-50.6% (M=28.9%, SD=8.9%). 13 operators traversed all 3 phases with dramatically divergent trajectories (+31.5pp to -38.3pp).
categories.claims: Early-entry operators get broader skill diets (motor + judgment + problem-solving). Late-entry operators get narrower (judgment only). Individual trajectories diverge dramatically within same organization.
categories.boundaries: Single organization (Kindred/JointBot). Observational data. Skill-building time ≠ skill acquisition.

events.summary: Scoping phase: analysis complete, three candidate framings
events.detail: Preliminary analysis of JointBot HRI telemetry at individual operator level (disaggregated from Developmental Uncertainty paper which uses aggregate data). Striking finding: among 13 operators who worked through all 3 technology phases, trajectories diverge dramatically — jessie went from 19.8% to 51.3% skill-building time while david.hallock went from 45.6% to 7.3%. Entry cohort determines skill composition: late joiners got fundamentally narrower skill diets (GRASPING and REACTING near zero). Analysis scripts complete, figures generated, but no manuscript drafted. LinkedIn data collection planned but not executed.
events.trigger: Theory-forge pipeline applied to disaggregated JointBot data
